model.description: xAI • Large reasoning model designed for real-time information access, coding, and general problem solving.

pick.rationale: Purdue ranks 8th in both KenPom and Torvik with an elite top-2 adjusted offense and solid defense from a tough Big Ten schedule, creating a clear quality gap over Miami (FL) at 30th and 35th respectively despite the Hurricanes' strong 25-8 record from the ACC. Miami advanced past Missouri as a 7-seed but lacks the offensive firepower to consistently counter Purdue's efficiency, with no major injuries reported for either team that would alter the outlook. While analysts like Jay Williams have noted Miami as a potential challenge to Purdue's bracket path, the metrics show only a modest upset risk and no fully credible path for the underdog to prevail in this neutral-site Round of 32 game.

reasoningStep.title: Advanced Ratings and Matchup Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Purdue holds a substantial edge in efficiency metrics over Miami, supporting a high-confidence pick for the higher seed.
evidence: Purdue: KenPom #8 (adj EM 30.5, adjO #2), Torvik #8 (adjOE 132.6 #1); Miami: KenPom #30 (adj EM 20.7), Torvik #35
No significant injuries found via searches; prior pick showed Miami at 67 confidence over Missouri while Purdue was 96 over Queens

result.text: ESPN analyst says Miami can challenge Purdue in NCAA tournament 2026 Skip to main content Home Schedule NFL News Draft Fantasy Odds Betting Newsletter USA TODAY Sports Jay Bilas said Purdue's March Madness path was easy. What about Miami? Dan Rorabaugh USA TODAY NETWORK - Florida Show Caption Hide Caption Who’s ready to put on the glass slipper this March? Two underdog teams that could make a Cinderella run this March Madness are Saint Louis and Miami (Ohio). Bracketologists seemed to be writing off Miami after their fate was revealed in the 2026 NCAA men's basketball tournament . At least one ESPN analyst came to the Hurricanes' defense. The Canes received a No. 7 seed in the West Region and will face No. 10 Missouri in what's effectively a road game in their first-round matchup in St. Louis . If they win to continue their March Madness expedition, Miami would likely get No. 2 Purdue in the second round (unless 15-seed Queens University pulls off a massive upset). The CBS selection show didn't spend much time discussing UM and its prospects in the bracket, perhaps because their lead-in was the Boilermakers knocking off Michigan to win the Big Ten tournament championship. And it seemed ESPN's Bracketology show would go on the same route. Buy Miami NCAA tournament tickets Jay Bilas got the first shot at analyzing the West Region and while he thought top-seeded Arizona had a tough draw, he felt differently about the bottom half of the bracket. "I think Purdue has a pretty decent draw here," Bilas said. In fairness, he did go on to say "clearly Miami-Missouri, that winner's not going to be an easy second-round game" before expounding on the weaknesses of No. 3-seed Gonzaga and No. 6 BYU. But then former North Carolina guard Jay Williams stepped in to challenge the Duke alum. "I think Purdue will be OK, but Miami's a really good team," Williams said. "Miami's played well. Jai Lucas has done one heck of a job with this team, obviously Tre Donaldson is a guy who can knock

modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.
